nativeRange.habitat: Elizabeth's Dwarf Cichlid inhabits slow-moving blackwater streams, igarapés (shallow forest-floor brooks), and floodplain pools within the Rio Negro basin of Brazil. The bottom is typically covered in a thick layer of fallen leaves and woody debris, shaded by overhanging riparian vegetation. Water chemistry is extreme by most aquarium standards: hardness is near zero and pH can fall below 5.0, producing the dark, tannin-stained water characteristic of Amazonian blackwater environments.

decorPreferences: deep leaf-litter bed (Indian almond, oak, catappa leaves)
small caves and coconut shells for female spawning sites
driftwood and roots for line-of-sight breaks
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
peat filtration or RO water to maintain blackwater chemistry
tannin-stained water via botanicals or driftwood

diet.notes: Elizabeth's Dwarf Cichlid is a carnivore that feeds primarily on small invertebrates in the wild, including microcrustaceans, chironomid larvae (bloodworms), and tiny insects gleaned from leaf litter. In the aquarium, live and frozen foods — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, microworms, grindal worms, and small bloodworms — are strongly preferred and should form the core of the diet. Once settled, many individuals will accept high-quality micro-pellets and crushed cichlid pellets, though variety in the diet is important for long-term condition.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, ember tetras) as dither fish
small corydoras or otocinclus as bottom companions in larger tanks
small rasboras tolerant of acidic water
dwarf freshwater shrimp in species-only or low-aggression setups (with caution near spawning females)
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species (inter-species aggression and hybridization risk)
boisterous or nippy fish (tiger barbs, serpae tetras)
fast surface-dwelling species that outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Apistogramma elizabethae; check current URL at fishbase.se/summary/Apistogramma-elizabethae.html for updated asset path.

summary: Elizabeth’s Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma elizabethae) is a rare Rio Negro species prized for its lyretail caudal fin and vivid blue-gold coloration in males. Females shift to bright yellow when guarding a brood. The species requires very soft, highly acidic blackwater chemistry and is suited to experienced keepers.
careNotes: Apistogramma elizabethae belongs to the elizabethae species-group within the subgenus Apistogramma and is endemic to the upper and middle Rio Negro drainage of Brazil. It inhabits blackwater biotopes where pH can fall below 5.0 and hardness is essentially zero; deviating significantly from these conditions leads to chronic stress, immune suppression, and shortened lifespan. RO water remineralized to 0–2 dGH, supplemented with tannin sources (Indian almond leaves, catappa bark, peat filtration), is strongly recommended. Target pH 4.5–5.5 for breeding and long-term health. Temperature should be kept at 26–28°C for most of the year with a modest seasonal drop to 24–25°C to stimulate spawning. A well-established, biologically mature tank is essential before introduction; new tanks with unstable water chemistry will cause rapid decline. Frequent partial water changes (20–30% weekly) with RO or soft rain water are required to maintain dissolved organic levels without spiking pH. The species is naturally haremic: keep as a single male with two or more females in tanks of 80 L or more, with clear visual barriers (driftwood, leaf litter, caves) so subordinate females can avoid a dominant spawning female. Males are larger (up to 8 cm) with lyre-shaped caudal lobes and iridescent blue-gold flanks; females remain at 3.5–4.5 cm and adopt bright yellow coloration when brooding. Dither fish (cardinal tetras, pencilfish) reduce timidity and provide behavioral cues for the cichlids that the environment is safe. The species is infrequently available in the hobby and typically sourced from specialist breeders or wild-caught imports.
breedingNotes: Elizabeth’s Dwarf Cichlid is a cave-spawning, haremic Apistogramma. Spawning is triggered by gradually lowering temperature to 24–25°C, increasing water changes, and introducing live foods. The female selects a small cave, coconut half, or dense leaf-litter pocket as a spawning site and deposits 30–80 eggs on the ceiling or walls. The female alone guards the eggs and fry aggressively, often confining the male to the tank perimeter; removing the male after spawning reduces female stress in smaller setups. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C, with free-swimming fry appearing 5–7 days later. Fry are small but accept baby brine shrimp nauplii and microworms from the first week. The female provides extended brood care for 3–4 weeks. Rearing fry in blackwater conditions matching the parents is critical; hard or alkaline water causes high fry mortality. Breeding difficulty is rated advanced owing to the precise water chemistry requirements and the low availability of conditioned stock.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma elizabethae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Rio Negro), water parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, temperature 24–30°C, hardness near zero), adult size (males to 8 cm, females to 4.5 cm), blackwater biotope requirements, haremic social structure, cave-spawning behaviour, and diet (microcrustaceans and invertebrates). Species-group placement (elizabethae-group) confirmed.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma elizabethae
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (males to 8.0 cm TL), native range (Rio Negro, Brazil), diet classification (invertivore/carnivore), and temperature range. Confirmed IUCN status and distribution data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guide used for hobbyist-oriented tank size, feeding, and compatibility context. Confirmed blackwater chemistry importance, cave-spawning setup, dither fish recommendation, and haremic grouping for the genus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Apistogramma species profiles
additional.notes: Supplementary source for dwarf cichlid blackwater biotope requirements, elizabethae species-group notes, breeding challenge level, and lyretail male morphology description.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dwarf Cichlids of the Rio Negro
additional.notes: Supplementary field and aquarium account of Rio Negro Apistogramma species including A. elizabethae; used for biotope description, pH tolerance range (4.5–6.0 in aquarium conditions), and breeding notes on haremic cave-spawning strategy.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner with female-only brood care. Spawning requires strict blackwater conditions — pH 4.5–5.5 and near-zero hardness — to proceed and for fry to survive. Clutch size is typically 30–80 eggs; fry accept baby brine shrimp nauplii from the first week. The advanced difficulty rating reflects the precise water chemistry demands and the general scarcity of conditioned stock in the hobby.
